source.title: Memory-enhancing amygdala stimulation elicits gamma synchrony in the hippocampus.

confidence_rationale: Provides a circuit mechanism for HOW BLA influences hippocampal write strength: CA3-CA1 low gamma synchrony (30-55 Hz) coordinates membrane depolarization with incoming spikes to enable spike-timing dependent LTP. Directly reveals a mechanism for the encoding_gain effect at the electrophysiological level. Confidence moderated by use of electrical stimulation rather than naturalistic arousal.
failure_signatures: Electrical BLA stimulation may not faithfully replicate the NE-release pathway of natural arousal; gamma synchrony timing may differ under naturalistic z_harm_a conditions.
Does not quantify a gain factor; demonstrates memory facilitation for specific recently-encountered objects, not a generalized write-strength multiplier.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Brief post-encounter BLA electrical stimulation enhances 1-day object memory; stimulation elicits CA3-CA1 low gamma (30-55 Hz) synchrony; hippocampal spiking during stimulation reflects recent activity patterns; mechanism is coordinated spike-timing dependent plasticity at recently active synapses.
mapping.ree_translation: BLA-driven gamma synchrony is the neural implementation of MECH-074a's encoding_gain: the BLA amplifies hippocampal write strength by coordinating precise spike timing (STDP) at recently active synapses. In REE, encoding_gain > 1 represents enhanced synaptic tagging at the write site.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Electrical stimulation model precludes testing graded arousal inputs; gamma synchrony is a temporal coordination mechanism, distinct from a purely scalar gain multiplier as implemented in REE.
mapping.source_context: Male Long-Evans rats, novel object exploration, bilateral BLA electrodes, in vivo hippocampal LFP recording.

confidence_components.notes: Solid journal; gamma synchrony mechanism is plausible and fits theta-gamma coupling framework, but stimulation != arousal.
